questions.question: ARIMA requires the input series to be:
questions.explanation: ARIMA (AutoRegressive Integrated Moving Average) requires stationarity. The 'I' (integrated) component handles differencing to achieve it.

questions.question: MAPE measures:
questions.explanation: MAPE = mean(|actual - predicted| / |actual|) * 100. A scale-free metric good for comparing across datasets.

questions.question: In ARIMA(p, d, q), 'd' represents:
questions.explanation: 'd' is the order of differencing needed to make the series stationary. d=1 means first differencing.

questions.question: Prophet was designed for:
options: Business time series with holidays and multiple seasonalities
questions.explanation: Meta's Prophet handles daily/weekly/yearly seasonality and holiday effects automatically with minimal configuration.

questions.question: The Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) test checks for:
questions.explanation: Low ADF p-value (< 0.05) rejects the unit root hypothesis — indicating stationarity.

questions.question: ACF (Autocorrelation Function) plots help determine:
options: The lag at which correlation drops to near-zero — guiding MA terms
questions.explanation: ACF shows correlation at each lag. It helps choose the 'q' (MA order) in ARIMA — q is where ACF cuts off.

questions.question: LSTM networks are preferred for time series when:
options: Fewer than 100 data points are available
questions.explanation: LSTMs can learn long-range dependencies through gated memory cells — useful for complex sequential patterns OLS/ARIMA cannot capture.

questions.question: Cross-validation for time series differs from standard k-fold because:
options: It must preserve temporal order — future data cannot train a model evaluated on past data
questions.explanation: Time series CV uses expanding or rolling windows, always training on past data and validating on future data.

questions.question: Seasonal differencing removes:
options: The seasonal pattern at lag s (e.g., 12 for monthly)
questions.explanation: Seasonal differencing: y(t) - y(t-s) removes the periodic component, useful before fitting SARIMA.

questions.question: XGBoost with lag features can be used for time series by:
options: Treating it as a sequence prediction problem using RNNs
Converting the series to a supervised problem using lagged values as features
Using the raw series as target
Applying it directly without feature engineering
questions.explanation: Creating features lag_1, lag_2 … lag_n from the series allows standard ML models to treat forecasting as regression.
